introSection: Orient Express was a steel roller coaster built by Arrow Development formerly located at Worlds of Fun in Kansas City, Missouri, USA. It operated from April 4, 1980 to October 26, 2003.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Orient Express began operation on April 4, 1980.

On October 29, 2003, Worlds of Fun announced that they would be removing Orient Express due to excessive maintenance costs and plans for future development. The ride's last operating day was October 26, 2003.

One of the ride's lead cars was donated to the National Roller Coaster Museum.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
The loops interlocked. A short brake section followed the first vertical loop. The second vertical loop led directly into the batwing.

The right side of station had a chicken exit that guests could take if they chickened out at the last minute. The sign for the chicken exit now resides in the station for Timber Wolf.

sections.title: Ride experience
sections.content: After leaving the station, the train enters a tunnel, makes a right turn and climbs the  chain lift hill. Riders hit a right turn and drop at. The train makes its way to the turnaround, where it makes a left turn. After that, riders approach the first vertical loop, which is the top interlocking loop. Following the first loop, the train hits the block brake. Riders make a right turn and dive into the second vertical loop, which is the bottom interlocking loop. After completing the interlocking loops, riders head straight into a batwing, where the train enters a backwards half loop, then a regular half loop and then exits in the opposite direction. The train then hits a helix over a small pond. After a right turn, riders hit the final brake run as they return to the station.

sections.title: Incidents
sections.content:
* Two trains collided in June 1987, injuring nine riders and leaving some passengers stranded above the ground for several hours.
 * On July 17, 1999, metal fatigue originating in the support structure caused two cars to derail, leaving riders stranded above the ground. Nobody was seriously injured.

fullText:
Orient Express was a steel roller coaster built by Arrow Development formerly located at Worlds of Fun in Kansas City, Missouri, USA. It operated from April 4, 1980 to October 26, 2003.

Orient Express began operation on April 4, 1980.

On October 29, 2003, Worlds of Fun announced that they would be removing Orient Express due to excessive maintenance costs and plans for future development. The ride's last operating day was October 26, 2003.

One of the ride's lead cars was donated to the National Roller Coaster Museum.

The loops interlocked. A short brake section followed the first vertical loop. The second vertical loop led directly into the batwing.

The right side of station had a chicken exit that guests could take if they chickened out at the last minute. The sign for the chicken exit now resides in the station for Timber Wolf.

After leaving the station, the train enters a tunnel, makes a right turn and climbs the  chain lift hill. Riders hit a right turn and drop at. The train makes its way to the turnaround, where it makes a left turn. After that, riders approach the first vertical loop, which is the top interlocking loop. Following the first loop, the train hits the block brake. Riders make a right turn and dive into the second vertical loop, which is the bottom interlocking loop. After completing the interlocking loops, riders head straight into a batwing, where the train enters a backwards half loop, then a regular half loop and then exits in the opposite direction. The train then hits a helix over a small pond. After a right turn, riders hit the final brake run as they return to the station.

Red track and supports.




 * Two trains collided in June 1987, injuring nine riders and leaving some passengers stranded above the ground for several hours.
 * On July 17, 1999, metal fatigue originating in the support structure caused two cars to derail, leaving riders stranded above the ground. Nobody was seriously injured.
